turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has our opinion this morning in case 15-1406, Goodyear Tire & Rubber versus Haeger.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: When a party acts in bad faith during litigation, a federal court has the inherent authority to sanction that behavior. One way to do that is to order the misbehaving litigants to pay the other side's legal fees. In this case, we hold that such a fee award must be limited to the expenses that are actually caused by the misconduct. This lawsuit began after the Haegar family got into a serious motor home accident. The family sued Goodyear alleging that one of its tires was responsible. During the litigation, the Haegars repeatedly asked Goodyear to turn over testing data for the tire model called the G159, but Goodyear claims not to have any such data. Only after settling the case did the Haegars learned that Goodyear had withheld some important test results relating to the G159. At that point, the Haegars sought sanctions for discovery fraud asking the district court to order Goodyear to reimburse the attorneys fees and costs that they had paid while litigating the case. The district court obliged and awarded the Haegars all the fees they had paid after Goodyear's first dishonest discovery response, $2.7 million in total. In the district court's view, that kind of all-fees award was appropriate in egregious case like this one. Even though in the usual case, sanctions had to be limited to the fees resulting from litigation misconduct. The Ninth Circuit affirms the district court's ruling, but we think both courts were wrong. When a court awards attorney's fees as a sanction for litigation misconduct using the ordinary procedures of civil litigation, it has to limit that award to the amount needed to compensate the wronged party for its losses. That is true in egregious cases, as well as in not so egregious ones. And that means pretty much by definition that the court must establish a causal link between the litigation misconduct and the legal fees awarded. That kind of causal connection is best framed as a But For test. A court may award only those fees that the injured party would not have paid but for the misconduct or said the other way around, when the injured party would have paid fees in the absence of the misconduct, then the Court cannot award those fees as a sanction. That But For standard generally calls for a court to evaluate each litigation expense, say for drafting a motion or taking a deposition and to decide whether that expense would or would not have been incurred in the absence of the misconduct. But we do not intend to turn courts into green eyeshade accountants. Fee shifting is a kind of rough justice, so courts may make estimates in crafting awards and in exceptional case, they may even shift all of a parties' fees because all of them resulted from the misconduct. But for reasons spelled out in the opinion, this is not one of those exceptional cases. So the district court's all-inclusive $2.7 million award cannot stand. We send the case back to the lower courts for further consideration of what award is appropriate under the standard we have set out. The opinion is unanimous except that Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of the case.
